scenario_text: A multilingual policy copilot used by compliance and operations teams usually retrieves the right internal standards, but adversarial review found that when two regional policy variants conflict it often drafts a confident reconciliation instead of abstaining or escalating. The issue appears most often when older English guidance and newer Arabic policy updates are both retrieved and the model treats them as mutually compatible.

scenario_context.operating_context: Enterprise policy copilot used across control-sensitive workflows

event_path_summary: The copilot behaves unsafely in ambiguous policy-conflict situations because it fails to abstain and overstates certainty.
primary_driver: Calibration and abstention weakness in an enterprise AI assistant
main_business_consequence: Users may rely on unsupported guidance in control-sensitive decisions unless the system is better evaluated and bounded.

valid_risks.title: Unsafe AI decision-support behaviour
valid_risks.why_valid: The assistant gives confident guidance when it should defer or escalate.

valid_risks.title: Insufficient ambiguity-handling evaluation
valid_risks.why_valid: The issue appears in conflict cases that the current evaluation design did not catch.

valid_risks.title: Calibration weakness in multilingual policy support
valid_risks.why_valid: Confidence is not aligned with true reliability when policy variants conflict.

invalid_risks.title: Regulatory filing failure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No filing event is the primary trigger.

invalid_risks.title: External hacker intrusion
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No cyber compromise caused the behaviour.

invalid_risks.title: Cross-border transfer breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The issue is not unlawful data movement.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier delivery miss
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No supply-chain event drives the scenario.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The initiating problem is unsafe model behaviour in a control-sensitive use case, which makes AI / Model Risk primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Compliance is adjacent because policy obligations are involved, but the failure begins with model behaviour and evaluation weakness.
Operational teams are affected, yet the event is not a generic process breakdown.
No cyber or privacy event initiates the scenario.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating policy-assistant scenarios as Compliance by default instead of recognising abstention and calibration failure.

scenario_text: Support engineers copied live troubleshooting logs from a UAE healthcare-adjacent workflow into an overseas diagnostics environment to speed root-cause analysis. The data was not publicly exposed, but the transfer happened before the required assessment and contractual safeguards were completed, and the copied logs still contained direct personal identifiers.

scenario_context.operating_context: Cross-border diagnostics and troubleshooting for regulated digital services

event_path_summary: Personal data was moved across borders without the required safeguards during support troubleshooting.
primary_driver: Transfer-governance failure in handling regulated support data
main_business_consequence: The organisation must contain the copied data, complete remediation, and reassess whether the transfer was lawful.

valid_risks.title: Cross-border transfer governance failure
valid_risks.why_valid: The event is explicitly about regulated data moving internationally without required safeguards.

valid_risks.title: Personal-data handling weakness in support operations
valid_risks.why_valid: The copied logs still contain direct identifiers in a support workflow.

valid_risks.title: Privacy control design gap in diagnostics workflow
valid_risks.why_valid: The process allowed live regulated data to be moved before approvals were complete.

invalid_risks.title: Data breach by attackers
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile compromise or theft caused the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Vendor insolvency
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No supplier financial distress is involved.

invalid_risks.title: Cloud availability outage
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No continuity or availability trigger is central.

invalid_risks.title: Model hallucination risk
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No AI behaviour failure initiated the event.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The central issue is governed handling of personal data and cross-border safeguards, which makes Data Governance / Privacy primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Compliance is adjacent because legal obligations apply, but the event starts with data handling and transfer governance.
Operational troubleshooting pressure is secondary to the privacy failure.
No cyber compromise or third-party failure is described as the primary event.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Classifying cross-border privacy wording as generic Compliance or Cyber instead of a data-governance event.

scenario_text: A key hardware vendor repeatedly misses revised shipment dates for specialist cooling units needed in several regional data-centre fit-outs. The vendor remains solvent and cooperative, but each slip pushes dependent installation windows, field-engineering bookings, and customer activation milestones further out.

scenario_context.operating_context: Regional infrastructure deployment with specialist manufactured components

event_path_summary: Repeated vendor shipment slippage is delaying dependent deployment work across a physical delivery chain.
primary_driver: Supplier delivery underperformance against committed dates
main_business_consequence: Installations and customer activations slip because required components do not arrive when planned.

valid_risks.title: Inbound material delivery slippage
valid_risks.why_valid: The event is defined by late arrival of required physical components.

valid_risks.title: Supplier dependency delay risk
valid_risks.why_valid: Downstream work cannot proceed because one vendor keeps slipping dates.

valid_risks.title: Schedule fragility from delivery commitments
valid_risks.why_valid: Dependent engineering and rollout milestones are pulled by the supplier delay.

invalid_risks.title: Third-party governance breach as primary lens
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Supplier oversight matters, but the initiating event is the physical delivery miss.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber compromise of deployment systems
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No attack is driving the slippage.

invalid_risks.title: Transformation design failure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Programme pressure is downstream of the supplier delivery miss.

invalid_risks.title: Fraudulent invoice payment
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No finance fraud event is involved.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The scenario is driven by late physical supply into dependent deployment work, which is Supply Chain.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational delay is a consequence of the supplier miss, not the root cause.
Third-Party is adjacent because a vendor is involved, but the issue is not primarily governance-only.
Transformation Delivery may feel nearby because milestones slip, yet the mechanism is supply continuity.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Calling supplier-led rollout delay Operational or Transformation Delivery instead of Supply Chain.

scenario_text: A support partner retained standing privileged access into several production systems after its scope was narrowed to a non-critical service desk role. Reviews found the access path was shared across teams, poorly segregated, and still available to subcontractor personnel who no longer had a documented business need. No compromise has been confirmed, but the external access posture is materially wider than approved.

scenario_context.operating_context: External support access into production environments with regulated control expectations

event_path_summary: External support access is over-privileged and weakly governed even though no compromise event has occurred.
primary_driver: Weak oversight and segregation of third-party privileged access
main_business_consequence: The organisation carries inherited control risk because external accounts retain broader reach than the approved service model requires.

valid_risks.title: Over-privileged vendor access risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The event is about external accounts having broader access than approved.

valid_risks.title: Weak third-party access governance
valid_risks.why_valid: Segregation and oversight over the partner access path are inadequate.

valid_risks.title: Inherited control exposure from subcontractor access
valid_risks.why_valid: Subcontractor personnel still retain access without a clear current business need.

invalid_risks.title: Confirmed cyber intrusion
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No compromise or hostile use of the access path is described.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier delivery delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No physical delivery chain event is involved.

invalid_risks.title: Regulatory filing breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No filing obligation is the primary event.

invalid_risks.title: Site-level continuity outage
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No service interruption or facility disruption initiated the problem.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The main issue is weak governance over an external access path, which makes Third-Party primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Cyber is adjacent because privileged access is mentioned, but there is no live compromise event.
Compliance matters because outsourcing controls apply, yet the initiating problem is partner-governance weakness.
Operational effects are possible, but not the event path itself.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Over-classifying vendor-access weakness as Cyber whenever privileged access is mentioned.

scenario_text: A specialist engineering team has absorbed repeated weekend bid work, urgent design reviews, and on-call support while also onboarding a large cohort of new hires. Attrition has started to rise, newer staff report weak coaching, and delivery increasingly depends on a shrinking core of experienced architects who are carrying the most complex approvals and client escalations.

scenario_context.operating_context: Scaling specialist engineering capability under sustained workload concentration

event_path_summary: Workload concentration, weak onboarding, and attrition are creating people-led fragility in a critical specialist team.
primary_driver: Fatigue, retention pressure, and critical-staff dependency
main_business_consequence: Delivery resilience and knowledge depth weaken because too much capability sits with a shrinking group of experienced staff.

valid_risks.title: Workforce fatigue and staffing weakness
valid_risks.why_valid: The team is showing sustained workload and resilience pressure.

valid_risks.title: Critical-staff dependency
valid_risks.why_valid: Too much delivery and approval capability sits with too few experienced individuals.

valid_risks.title: Onboarding and capability-development weakness
valid_risks.why_valid: Newer staff are not being brought up quickly enough to reduce concentration risk.

invalid_risks.title: Site safety incident
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No actual injury or site-hazard event is described.

invalid_risks.title: DDoS attack
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No cyber event initiated the problem.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier concentration risk
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The scenario is about internal workforce resilience, not vendors.

invalid_risks.title: Regulatory transfer breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No privacy or transfer event is central.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The initiating event is people-capability strain and dependency on a shrinking expert core, which is People / Workforce.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational delay is a consequence of the workforce issue, not the root cause.
Transformation Delivery is adjacent if programmes are affected, but the immediate event is people resilience.
HSE should not be primary because no actual safety incident is described.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating workforce strain as generic Operational pressure instead of People / Workforce.

scenario_text: An investor-facing sustainability update stated that Scope 2 emissions fell materially after workloads were moved to a greener cloud mix, but assurance prep found the renewable energy attributes cited in the narrative did not match the actual geography of several production workloads. The claim may still be directionally true, yet the published reduction cannot currently be evidenced on the basis used in the draft disclosure.

scenario_context.operating_context: Enterprise sustainability disclosure for cloud and digital operations

event_path_summary: A public sustainability claim cannot be substantiated because disclosure evidence does not match the real operating footprint.
primary_driver: Weak substantiation of an emissions-reduction claim
main_business_consequence: The group faces disclosure-credibility and reputational pressure unless the claim is corrected or better evidenced.

valid_risks.title: Sustainability-claim substantiation risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The core issue is whether the public claim can be evidenced.

valid_risks.title: Carbon accounting methodology weakness
valid_risks.why_valid: The reduction logic does not currently match the actual operating footprint.

valid_risks.title: ESG disclosure credibility risk
valid_risks.why_valid: Stakeholder trust could be damaged if the published claim is overstated.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber breach of reporting systems
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile compromise caused the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Routine finance-control error
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is not a transactional finance event.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier delivery slippage
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No supply-chain delay caused the claim gap.

invalid_risks.title: Office stationery restocking delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is an ordinary workplace task and unrelated to the event.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The event is about the supportability of a sustainability claim and the integrity of ESG disclosure, which makes ESG primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Compliance is adjacent because disclosure rules apply, but the root issue is the claim itself.
Operational details about workload placement matter only because they affect the ESG claim.
Third-Party considerations are secondary even if cloud arrangements contribute to the footprint logic.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Flattening sustainability-disclosure issues into generic Compliance rather than ESG claim integrity.

scenario_text: A supplier review found that migrant workers at a sub-tier manufacturing site had paid recruitment fees and had passports held by labour agents during mobilisation. The direct supplier had passed standard audits, but deeper investigation showed the group’s due-diligence process was not reaching the labour-broker layer that shaped actual working conditions.

scenario_context.operating_context: Value-chain due diligence over specialised manufacturing suppliers

event_path_summary: Human-rights abuse exists in the supply chain because due diligence did not reach the labour-broker layer.
primary_driver: Forced-labour and worker-exploitation risk missed by supplier due diligence
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces remediation, stakeholder, and disclosure pressure because supply-chain labour conditions do not meet expected standards.

valid_risks.title: Forced-labour / modern-slavery exposure
valid_risks.why_valid: The event path is explicit worker exploitation in the supply chain.

valid_risks.title: Human-rights due-diligence failure
valid_risks.why_valid: Existing oversight did not reach the layer where the abuse occurred.

valid_risks.title: Supplier remediation and disclosure pressure
valid_risks.why_valid: The group must now respond to an ESG and human-rights issue in its value chain.

invalid_risks.title: Generic procurement delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is not a sourcing-timeline issue.

invalid_risks.title: Vendor access compromise
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No cyber or remote-access event caused the problem.

invalid_risks.title: Single-source dependency
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Supplier concentration is not the core event path.

invalid_risks.title: Accounts-payable fraud
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No finance or deception event is involved.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The primary event is human-rights abuse and forced-labour exposure in the value chain, which is ESG.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Third-Party is adjacent because suppliers are involved, but the event path is not generic vendor governance alone.
Compliance matters because due-diligence obligations apply, yet the root problem is the human-rights abuse itself.
People / Workforce is secondary because the workers are outside the organisation's own workforce.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Collapsing forced-labour wording into Procurement or Third-Party instead of ESG human-rights risk.

scenario_text: An externally shared climate update stated that value-chain emissions intensity fell after key suppliers shifted to lower-carbon inputs, but assurance review found that several supplier data packs used mismatched activity assumptions and outdated emission factors. The direction of travel may still be positive, yet the reported Scope 3 reduction cannot currently be reconciled to defensible underlying evidence.

scenario_context.operating_context: Supplier-emissions reporting and assurance over value-chain climate claims

event_path_summary: A public value-chain emissions claim cannot be substantiated because supplier data and calculation assumptions do not reconcile.
primary_driver: Weak substantiation of a Scope 3 reduction claim
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces disclosure-credibility pressure unless the value-chain emissions claim is corrected or better evidenced.

valid_risks.title: Scope 3 disclosure substantiation risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The main issue is whether the value-chain emissions claim can be supported.

valid_risks.title: Supplier-emissions data quality weakness
valid_risks.why_valid: Upstream data and factors do not currently support the published reduction.

valid_risks.title: Climate-reporting credibility pressure
valid_risks.why_valid: Stakeholder trust is at risk if the public climate statement is overstated.

invalid_risks.title: Generic supplier delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No delivery-chain disruption caused the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Routine financial-control error
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is not a disbursement or accounting-control event.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber breach of reporting systems
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile compromise is described.

invalid_risks.title: Internal-only policy reminder
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The problem is an externally relied-on claim, not just internal process housekeeping.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The event turns on whether a public climate claim can be evidenced, which makes ESG primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Compliance is adjacent because assurance and disclosure rules apply, but the root issue is claim supportability.
Third-Party matters because suppliers provide data, yet the event path is the unsupported ESG claim.
Operational data handling matters only because it affects the climate disclosure.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating supplier-emissions evidence gaps as generic Compliance or Supply Chain instead of ESG disclosure integrity.

scenario_text: A sustainability-linked financing facility would achieve a favourable pricing step-down if the borrower met an emissions-intensity KPI this year, and investor materials already referenced the expected result. Assurance preparation then found that several energy-intensive sites had been excluded from the baseline used to support the claimed KPI outcome, leaving the margin-related sustainability statement materially under-evidenced.

scenario_context.operating_context: Financing-linked ESG KPI tracking and external stakeholder communications

event_path_summary: A financing-linked ESG KPI claim is under challenge because the evidence base excludes material operating activity.
primary_driver: Weak substantiation of a sustainability-linked KPI outcome
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces financing-credibility and disclosure pressure because the claimed KPI performance may be overstated.

valid_risks.title: Sustainability-linked KPI substantiation risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The issue is whether the KPI outcome can be evidenced credibly.

valid_risks.title: ESG disclosure-control weakness in financing communications
valid_risks.why_valid: External sustainability messaging moved ahead of the supportable evidence base.

valid_risks.title: Climate-metric baseline governance failure
valid_risks.why_valid: Material operations were omitted from the KPI baseline logic.

invalid_risks.title: Payment approval control failure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No payment-process breakdown triggered the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Counterparty default exposure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No insolvency or collectability event is involved.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber compromise of treasury systems
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile technology event caused the problem.

invalid_risks.title: Ordinary budgeting variance
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is not a routine finance-performance fluctuation.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The event is about supportability of an externally relied-on ESG KPI, which makes ESG primary even though financing is involved.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Financial is adjacent because pricing and treasury effects exist, but the initiating failure is ESG KPI substantiation.
Compliance matters because assurance and disclosure controls apply, yet the root issue is the claim itself.
Strategic context is secondary to the evidence gap.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Collapsing financing-linked sustainability KPI issues into ordinary Financial control language instead of ESG disclosure integrity.

scenario_text: External communications described the climate-transition roadmap as being on track after multiple energy-efficiency and low-carbon conversion milestones were said to have been completed across priority sites. A later internal review found that several milestone claims had been counted from planned or partially commissioned work rather than fully operating assets, so the public transition statement is ahead of the actual implementation status.

scenario_context.operating_context: Public reporting and internal tracking of transition-plan milestones

event_path_summary: A public transition-progress claim cannot be fully supported because milestone completion has been overstated.
primary_driver: Transition-plan milestone overstatement and weak evidence discipline
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces disclosure-credibility pressure because public transition messaging is ahead of the actual asset-conversion status.

valid_risks.title: Transition-plan disclosure credibility risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The main issue is whether the claimed climate-transition progress is supportable.

valid_risks.title: Climate-milestone evidence governance weakness
valid_risks.why_valid: Planned or partially live work was counted as complete in the disclosure logic.

valid_risks.title: Overstatement of sustainability progress
valid_risks.why_valid: External stakeholders may be relying on a transition claim that is ahead of reality.

invalid_risks.title: Programme delay as the primary risk
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Programme slippage is context, but the event is the unsupported public claim.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber outage
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No technology disruption caused the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Procurement concentration risk
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No supplier-dependency event is the primary driver.

invalid_risks.title: Routine health and safety incident
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No safety event is central.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The scenario is fundamentally about whether external transition claims can be evidenced, which makes ESG primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Strategic and programme implications exist, but the root event is disclosure overstatement.
Compliance is adjacent because reporting controls apply, yet the unsupported climate claim is the central issue.
Transformation Delivery is not primary because delivery slippage matters only because it affects the public claim.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating transition-claim overstatement as generic strategy or programme drift instead of ESG disclosure risk.

scenario_text: A multilingual grievance channel received repeated complaints that migrant workers in a sub-tier service provider had paid recruitment fees, surrendered passports to labour agents, and could not access remedy quickly after raising concerns. Management had supplier-code language in place, but remediation tracking showed the escalation path did not reach the labour-broker layer fast enough to stop the abuse.

scenario_context.operating_context: Human-rights due diligence, grievance handling, and remediation escalation across sub-tier labour channels

event_path_summary: Worker abuse is being surfaced through grievances, but remediation and escalation are not reaching the labour-broker layer quickly enough.
primary_driver: Human-rights abuse and delayed remediation in the labour-broker layer
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces remediation, disclosure, and stakeholder pressure because the grievance process is not containing supply-chain labour abuse effectively.

valid_risks.title: Forced-labour / worker-exploitation exposure
valid_risks.why_valid: The event path is explicit labour abuse and coercive worker treatment.

valid_risks.title: Human-rights grievance and remediation failure
valid_risks.why_valid: The grievance mechanism did not trigger effective remediation quickly enough.

valid_risks.title: Supply-chain escalation weakness over labour brokers
valid_risks.why_valid: Oversight did not reach the layer where the abuse was occurring.

invalid_risks.title: Generic procurement process gap
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is not mainly a sourcing-cycle issue.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier delivery underperformance
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No physical delivery miss caused the event.

invalid_risks.title: Vendor access weakness
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No cyber or system-access issue initiated the problem.

invalid_risks.title: Accounts-payable fraud
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No deception or payment-release event is involved.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The primary event is human-rights abuse and failed remediation, which makes ESG primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Third-Party is adjacent because suppliers and brokers are involved, but the event path is abuse and remedy rather than generic vendor governance.
Compliance matters because due-diligence duties apply, yet the root issue is the worker harm itself.
People / Workforce is secondary because the affected workers sit in the value chain, not the organisation's core workforce.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Reducing grievance-led human-rights abuse into Third-Party governance instead of ESG worker-protection risk.

scenario_text: A responsible-sourcing review found that a supplier had passed recent audits, yet remediation trackers showed excessive overtime, withheld documents, and poor accommodation controls continued at a labour-contractor site supporting a critical product line. The issue is no longer whether the supplier has a policy; it is whether worker-protection controls and follow-through are actually stopping the abuse.

scenario_context.operating_context: Remediation oversight over labour contractors in a critical supply chain

event_path_summary: Labour abuse persists because remediation follow-through and worker-protection controls are not effective enough at contractor level.
primary_driver: Weak remediation effectiveness over supply-chain labour abuse
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces stakeholder and disclosure pressure because corrective action is not yet containing the worker-harm risk.

valid_risks.title: Persistent labour-abuse exposure despite remediation
valid_risks.why_valid: The event path is continuing worker harm, not just policy existence.

valid_risks.title: Human-rights remediation effectiveness gap
valid_risks.why_valid: Corrective action is not resolving the abuse quickly enough.

valid_risks.title: Supplier oversight weakness over contractor welfare
valid_risks.why_valid: Controls at contractor level are still not protecting workers adequately.

invalid_risks.title: Single-source dependency
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No sourcing concentration issue is driving the event.

invalid_risks.title: Generic operational backlog
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is not primarily a throughput or scheduling problem.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber compromise
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile technology event caused the harm.

invalid_risks.title: Payment-control failure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No finance-control event is involved.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The scenario remains about worker welfare and human-rights remediation, which keeps ESG primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Third-Party is adjacent because contractors and suppliers are involved, but the event path is not generic vendor governance.
Compliance duties matter, yet the root issue is whether the abuse is actually being stopped.
People / Workforce is secondary because the affected workers are in the external labour chain.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Stopping at supplier-policy language and missing that the real event is failed remediation of ongoing worker abuse.

scenario_text: A public sustainability report stated that water-reuse targets and landfill-diversion performance were being met at key facilities, but reconciliation work later found bypass events, contractor manifests, and utility-meter data had not been aligned to the figures used in the disclosure pack. The environmental ambition remains intact, yet the published performance claim cannot currently be supported on the stated basis.

scenario_context.operating_context: Environmental performance reporting for facility operations

event_path_summary: A public environmental-performance claim cannot be substantiated because metering and waste evidence do not reconcile to the reported result.
primary_driver: Weak substantiation of a water and waste performance claim
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces disclosure-credibility pressure because the published environmental claim is ahead of the evidence base.

valid_risks.title: Environmental-claim substantiation risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The main issue is whether the public environmental claim can be evidenced.

valid_risks.title: Water and waste reporting control weakness
valid_risks.why_valid: Metering and manifest evidence were not aligned to the disclosed figures.

valid_risks.title: ESG disclosure credibility pressure
valid_risks.why_valid: Stakeholder trust could be damaged if the claim is overstated.

invalid_risks.title: Live pollution incident
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No spill or release event is the primary trigger.

invalid_risks.title: Routine facilities maintenance delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No scheduling issue caused the reporting gap.

invalid_risks.title: Cyberattack on metering systems
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile compromise is described.

invalid_risks.title: Generic procurement problem
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No sourcing-cycle weakness is central.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The event is about whether public environmental performance claims are supportable, which makes ESG primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: HSE is adjacent because environmental operations matter, but this is not a live spill or harm event.
Compliance matters because assurance and disclosure controls apply, yet the unsupported claim is the central issue.
Operational data handling matters only because it affects the ESG disclosure.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating unsupported environmental performance claims as HSE incident management rather than ESG disclosure credibility.

scenario_text: A district utility outage disabled one support site during quarter-end processing and forced teams to switch to remote work and alternate workspace arrangements. Core applications remained up, but several managers discovered the manual fallback steps for priority finance and contract tasks were incomplete, and relocation decisions stalled while teams waited for clarification.

scenario_context.operating_context: Corporate support operations responding to a site-level disruption

event_path_summary: A site-level disruption triggered continuity activation, but alternate workspace and manual fallback arrangements were not ready to execute cleanly.
primary_driver: Continuity-readiness gap during a live site disruption
main_business_consequence: Priority support processes face delay because relocation and fallback procedures are not operationalised well enough.

valid_risks.title: Continuity activation readiness gap
valid_risks.why_valid: The scenario is about whether the organisation can continue priority work after a site disruption.

valid_risks.title: Manual fallback weakness for critical tasks
valid_risks.why_valid: Priority activities do not have executable fallback steps.

valid_risks.title: Alternate operating arrangement fragility
valid_risks.why_valid: Relocation and remote-work arrangements are not ready enough for smooth execution.

invalid_risks.title: Malware outbreak
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The trigger is a utility outage, not a cyber event.

invalid_risks.title: Physical intrusion into the building
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Building access issues are secondary to the continuity event.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier delivery miss
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No supply-chain mechanism caused the disruption.

invalid_risks.title: Regulatory filing breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Any filing impact would be downstream, not the initiating event.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The central question is continuity of critical work through alternate arrangements after a site disruption, which is Business Continuity.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational execution issues are part of the continuity failure, not separate from it.
People preparation matters, but it is subordinate to the continuity event path.
Physical Security is adjacent only because site access is affected.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating site-outage scenarios as generic Operational issues rather than continuity activation problems.

scenario_text: A group-wide operating-model transformation is rolling out a common service workflow across cloud, data, and industrial-support teams. Pilots looked successful, but local exceptions for regulation, language, customer escalation commitments, and site-access constraints are now accumulating faster than the programme can absorb them, leaving teams unsure which version of the target state is still real.

scenario_context.operating_context: Rollout of a common operating model across diverse regional teams

event_path_summary: Local rollout exceptions are fragmenting the target state and undermining coherent programme delivery.
primary_driver: Target-state design drift and weak exception governance in a major rollout
main_business_consequence: The enterprise may fail to land one coherent operating model because the programme cannot hold local variations inside a controlled design.

valid_risks.title: Target-state fragmentation risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The future-state design is no longer staying coherent across rollout locations.

valid_risks.title: Transformation exception-governance failure
valid_risks.why_valid: Local variations are outrunning the programme's ability to control them.

valid_risks.title: Benefit-realisation pressure from delivery drift
valid_risks.why_valid: The promised operating model may not land because execution is fragmenting.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier logistics delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No physical-delivery chain event drives the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Warehouse aisle picking queue
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is a local fulfilment workload issue rather than the event.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber availability incident
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile attack caused the fragmentation.

invalid_risks.title: Retail store expansion choice
invalid_risks.why_invalid: This is a commercial portfolio choice rather than the event.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The primary failure lies in controlling and landing a complex transformation across exceptions, which makes Transformation Delivery primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational inconsistency is a downstream symptom of the failing programme.
Strategic relevance is real, but the event is not a strategy-choice failure.
Supply Chain and Third-Party may contribute constraints, yet they do not define the main event path.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Calling programme-design drift Operational because users feel the impact in day-to-day delivery.

scenario_text: Attackers locked several core file servers and engineering workspaces, then demanded payment in exchange for decrypting the environment. Teams can still communicate, but they cannot access working files cleanly, manual workarounds are building, and recovery options now depend on whether the encrypted estate can be restored from trusted backups.

scenario_context.operating_context: Enterprise digital workplace and shared-platform recovery under extortion pressure

event_path_summary: A ransomware-style encryption event is disrupting access to critical systems and creating extortion pressure.
primary_driver: Malicious encryption and recovery pressure after attacker access
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces service disruption, recovery strain, and decision pressure over restoration rather than a payment-control issue.

valid_risks.title: Ransomware and extortion exposure
valid_risks.why_valid: The main event is attacker-led encryption plus payment demand.

valid_risks.title: Recovery readiness under encrypted-system outage
valid_risks.why_valid: The practical question is how quickly trusted restoration can occur.

valid_risks.title: Operational disruption from malicious encryption
valid_risks.why_valid: The disruption is driven by the encryption event rather than a separate finance problem.

invalid_risks.title: Payment approval breakdown
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Payment is mentioned only as extortion pressure, not as the control failure.

invalid_risks.title: Receivables write-off risk
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No customer credit event is involved.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier delivery slippage
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No delivery dependency drives the disruption.

invalid_risks.title: Routine compliance remediation
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Governance follow-up is downstream of the cyber event.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The initiating event is malicious encryption and extortion, which makes Cyber primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Business Continuity is secondary because recovery matters, but the trigger is a cyberattack.
Operational disruption is a consequence of the encryption event.
Financial should not become primary merely because attackers demand payment.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Over-weighting the word payment and collapsing ransomware into finance-control language.

scenario_text: One of the group's largest customers entered formal insolvency protection after months of stretched payment behaviour, leaving a major receivables balance unlikely to be recovered in full. Commercial teams are still negotiating, but finance now expects a material write-off and refreshed provisioning unless recovery prospects improve quickly.

scenario_context.operating_context: Enterprise receivables management with concentrated customer exposure

event_path_summary: Customer insolvency is driving a receivables recovery shortfall and likely write-off.
primary_driver: Counterparty default and reduced collectability of a major receivable
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces direct financial loss and provisioning pressure because the customer may not pay.

valid_risks.title: Counterparty default exposure
valid_risks.why_valid: The core issue is customer insolvency and non-payment risk.

valid_risks.title: Receivables recovery shortfall
valid_risks.why_valid: Recovery prospects for the major balance have deteriorated materially.

valid_risks.title: Provisioning pressure from customer insolvency
valid_risks.why_valid: Finance must reflect the likely loss through updated provisioning.

invalid_risks.title: Payment approval fraud
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No deception or internal release manipulation caused the event.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber compromise of billing systems
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No hostile technology event initiated the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier shipment delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No supply-chain dependency caused the loss pressure.

invalid_risks.title: Data-retention breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No privacy or records-handling event is central.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The event is direct counterparty-credit deterioration and likely write-off, which makes Financial primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational follow-up is secondary to the financial exposure.
Compliance matters for provisioning and reporting, but does not define the primary event.
Fraud / Integrity should not be primary because there is no deceptive payment release.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating receivables loss scenarios as fraud or generic operational pressure instead of Financial.

scenario_text: A finance operations review found that emergency override access let a batch of duplicate supplier payments clear without the usual four-eyes approval. The duplicate releases were discovered quickly and there is no evidence of collusion or deception, but the payment-control design clearly failed under time pressure.

scenario_context.operating_context: Disbursement and approval control operation under urgent processing pressure

event_path_summary: Override access bypassed normal payment approvals and allowed duplicate disbursements.
primary_driver: Payment-control weakness and segregation-of-duties failure under emergency override
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces direct monetary leakage and control remediation because duplicate payments were released.

valid_risks.title: Payment approval control failure
valid_risks.why_valid: The main issue is the approval-control breakdown.

valid_risks.title: Duplicate-payment release exposure
valid_risks.why_valid: Funds were released twice because the control path failed.

valid_risks.title: Segregation-of-duties weakness in disbursements
valid_risks.why_valid: Emergency override bypassed the intended control separation.

invalid_risks.title: Collusive fraud ring
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The scenario explicitly says there is no evidence of deception or collusion.

invalid_risks.title: Cardholder data breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No cyber or payment-card compromise occurred.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier delivery delay
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No supplier continuity issue caused the payments.

invalid_risks.title: Routine ESG disclosure issue
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No sustainability or disclosure event is involved.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The event is a direct payment-control failure, which makes Financial primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Fraud / Integrity is adjacent but should not overtake the scenario without deception or collusion.
Compliance follow-up is secondary to the control failure itself.
Cyber should not be primary because no technology compromise caused the duplicate release.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Promoting every payment-control issue into Fraud / Integrity even when deception is absent.

scenario_text: Customer-support archives showed that identity-verification images and complaint recordings were kept well beyond the retention period stated in the privacy notice, even though the operational need had ended. The material is still internal and access-controlled, but the organisation can no longer explain a clear lawful basis for holding it for this long.

scenario_context.operating_context: Customer-service record handling and retention governance

event_path_summary: Sensitive customer records are being kept beyond the stated retention window without a defensible lawful basis.
main_business_consequence: The organisation faces remediation and supervisory pressure because it cannot justify the continued retention of sensitive records.

valid_risks.title: Records-retention governance failure
valid_risks.why_valid: The event is explicitly about holding data beyond the permitted period.

valid_risks.title: Lawful-basis weakness in customer-data archives
valid_risks.why_valid: The organisation cannot explain why the data is still being retained.

valid_risks.title: Privacy remediation pressure from excess retention
valid_risks.why_valid: The likely next step is data-governance remediation, not breach response.

invalid_risks.title: External data exfiltration
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The data remains internal and access-controlled.

invalid_risks.title: Cross-border transfer breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No international movement of the records caused the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier insolvency
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No third-party financial distress is involved.

invalid_risks.title: Platform outage
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No service-availability event is central.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The central event is over-retention of personal data without a lawful basis, which makes Data Governance / Privacy primary.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Compliance is adjacent because privacy obligations apply, but the event begins with data-retention governance.
Cyber should not be primary because there is no exposure or attack.
Legal / Contract pressure is secondary to the records-governance failure.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating retention problems as disclosure incidents even when no exposure is stated.
